# EDM
## Probability Flow ODE

Diffusion models involve several design choices. The first is the forward noising process, which defines the perturbation kernel $p(x_t \mid x_0)$ and includes formulations such as DDPM, VP-SDE, and VE-SDE. The second is the model parameterization, where the neural network may predict the score, noise $\epsilon$, or clean data $x_0$. The third is the sampling algorithm used to generate samples from the learned distribution, such as ancestral sampling, DDIM, or probability flow ODE solvers.

@karras_elucidating_2022 argued that we can use the same probability flow ODE for diffusion models trained using different design choices. Specifically, they claimed that the VE-SDE, corresponding to the perturbation kernel $p(y_t \mid y_0) = \mathcal{N}(y_t; y_0, \sigma^2(t)I)$ with $\sigma(t) = t$, where $y_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d$ represents samples from the data distribution $p_\text{data}$ and $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the corresponding noise-perturbed sample, induces probability paths that are less curved. This results in reduced discretization error and enables faster and more accurate sampling.

The reverse probability flow ODE corresponding to the perturbation kernel above is given by

$$
\mathrm{d}y = -\dot{\sigma}(t)\sigma(t)\nabla_y \log p(y;\sigma(t))\mathrm{d}t.
$$

Using the relationship given by Tweedie's formula,

$$
\nabla_y \log p(y;\sigma(t))
=
\frac{D(y;\sigma(t))-y}{\sigma(t)^2},
$$

the probability flow ODE can be written in terms of the denoised sample $D(y;\sigma)$ as

$$
\mathrm{d}y
=
-\dot{\sigma}(t)\sigma(t)
\frac{D(y;\sigma(t))-y}{\sigma(t)^2}
\mathrm{d}t.
$$

## Sampling a VP-SDE Model with the VE-SDE ODE

Suppose the diffusion model was trained using the VP-SDE process defined by the perturbation kernel

$$
p(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda(t)^2I),
$$

where $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a sample from the data distribution $p_\text{data}$.

To relate the VP-SDE formulation to the VE-SDE probability flow ODE, define

$$
y_t = \frac{x_t}{s(t)}.
$$

Then $y_t$ follows the perturbation kernel

$$
p(y_t \mid y_0) = \mathcal{N}(y_t;y_0,\sigma^2(t)I),
$$

with $x_0=y_0$, meaning that both processes are initialized from the same data sample.

### Relationship between $p_{x_t}(x)$ and $p_{y_t}(y)$

The marginal density of $x_t$ is

$$
p_{x_t}(x) = \int p(x_t \mid x_0)p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)dx_0.
$$

Substituting the VP-SDE perturbation kernel,

$$
p_{x_t}(x) = \int p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)\mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda^2(t)I)dx_0.
$$

Using

$$
\mathcal{N}(x_t;s(t)x_0,\lambda^2(t)I)
=
s(t)^{-d}\mathcal{N}\left(\frac{x_t}{s(t)};x_0,\frac{\lambda^2(t)}{s^2(t)}I\right),
$$

and defining

$$
\sigma(t)=\frac{\lambda(t)}{s(t)},
\qquad
y_t=\frac{x_t}{s(t)},
$$

we obtain

$$
p_{x_t}(x)
=
s(t)^{-d}\int p_{\mathrm{data}}(x_0)\mathcal{N}(y_t;x_0,\sigma^2(t)I)dx_0
=
s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y).
$$

### Relationship between $\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)$ and $\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)$

Using

$$
p_{x_t}(x)=s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y),
$$

we have

$$
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
\nabla_{x_t}\log\left(s(t)^{-d}p_{y_t}(y)\right)
=
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{y_t}(y).
$$

Since $y_t=x_t/s(t)$,

$$
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
\frac{1}{s(t)}
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y).
$$

Therefore,

$$
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
\frac{1}{s(t)}
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y),
$$

or equivalently,

$$
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
s(t)\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x).
$$

### Denoiser Expression for $\epsilon $ Parameterization

Suppose the model is trained for $\epsilon$ prediction, where the network is conditioned on the discrete time index and is denoted by $\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t)$.

The score of $p_{x_t}$ is given by

$$
\nabla_{x_t}\log p_{x_t}(x)
=
-\frac{1}{\lambda(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
$$

Using the relationship derived above,

$$
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
-\frac{s(t)}{\lambda(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
$$

Since $\sigma(t)=\lambda(t)/s(t)$,

$$
\nabla_{y_t}\log p_{y_t}(y)
=
-\frac{1}{\sigma(t)}
\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
$$



Using Tweedie's formula,

$$
D(y;\sigma)
=
y-\sigma\epsilon_\theta(x_t,(M-1)t).
$$

Substituting $x_t=s(t)y$ gives

$$
D(y;\sigma)
=
y-\sigma\epsilon_\theta(s(t)y,(M-1)t).
$$

<!-- ## References
::: {#refs}
::: -->
